article_title: Webb Reveals the Inner Workings of the 'Exposed Cranium' Nebula, a Dying Star's Brain-Shaped Shroud

content_preview.title: Webb Reveals the Inner Workings of the 'Exposed Cranium' Nebula, a Dying Star's Brain-Shaped Shroud
content_preview.summary: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope has captured the most detailed images yet of nebula PMR 1, nicknamed the Exposed Cranium, revealing a two-light-year-wide structure shaped by jets from a massive dying star roughly 5,000 light-years away in the constellation Vela.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA's James Webb Space Telescope has turned its infrared instruments on one of the sky's most visually striking objects: [nebula PMR 1](https://science.nasa.gov/missions/webb/nasas-webb-examines-cranium-nebula/), a cloud of gas and dust roughly 5,000 light-years away in the constellation Vela whose uncanny resemblance to a brain inside a transparent skull has earned it the nickname the "Exposed Cranium." The new observations, released by the Space Telescope Science Institute on Feb...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent science writing with clear structure, accessible language, and appropriate technical depth. Good use of Spitzer-to-Webb progression for context.
editor_notes.source_verification: NASA source (science.nasa.gov) confirms PMR 1 identity, NIRCam/MIRI instruments, dark lane, jets, Spitzer comparison, Feb 25 2026 release. Scientific American confirms 5,000 light-years distance, Vela constellation, star mass uncertainty, supernova vs white dwarf fate. Space.com article exists but content wasn't fully parseable; headline confirms topic. science.nasa.gov not on allowlist is a false positive — NASA is an authoritative primary source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All core claims verified. Distance (5,000 ly), constellation (Vela), instruments (NIRCam, MIRI), dark lane, jets, Spitzer comparison, and stellar fate uncertainty all confirmed across sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, descriptive, and engaging. No sensationalism despite the dramatic visual subject.
editor_notes.originality: No existing article on PMR 1 or the Exposed Cranium nebula in the archive.
recommendations: Add science.nasa.gov to source allowlist — it is NASA's official science portal
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science news submission with strong source attribution and factual accuracy. Approved.
